papers.title: Radio polarization from runaway star bowshocks-I. The general case
papers.author_names: M. V. Del Valle R. Santos-Lima M. Pohl

abstract.text: High velocity stars move through the interstellar medium with V > 30 km/s. When the star has powerful winds, under the appropriate conditions, the interaction of the wind with the interstellar material produces a system of shocks. The outer shock, called the bowshock, perturbs the ambient medium, heating and compressing the gas. The dust in the compressed bowshock cools, producing infrared radiation. This emission appears as extended coma-shape structures. The discovery of radio nonthermal emission from two stellar bowshock nebulae indicates that these sources might be accelerating electrons up to relativistic energies. The produced nonthermal radio emission is most probably synchrotron which has a high degree of polarization. In this work we model the synchrotron emission of runaway massive star bowshocks aiming to produce synthetic radio emission and polarization maps for two frequencies: 1.40 and 4.86 GHz. We model the interacting plasmas in a steady-state regime by means of magnetohydrodynamics simulations and we compute the injection and transport of the relativistic electrons in the diffusion approximation. We include in the model the most important depolarization effects. Our main conclusions are i) the effects of Faraday rotation within the source are important at the lowest frequency considered, ii) inferring the local magnetic field direction from polarization measurements only can be misleading, iii) thermal radio emission produced by ionized plasma within the bowshock structure and surroundings can surpass the polarized one for the considered frequencies, and iv) the contribution from the background electrons is minor.

brief.tldr: [theoretical modeling study] The paper develops a magnetohydrodynamics-based model of synchrotron emission and polarization in runaway massive star bowshocks, simulating the plasma structure and relativistic electron transport under diffusion approximation. It identifies key depolarization effects—particularly Faraday rotation—and demonstrates that thermal radio emission can dominate over polarized synchrotron radiation at 1.4–4.86 GHz, while local magnetic field inference from polarization alone is unreliable due to intrinsic depolarization and background contributions.

source_fields.title: Radio polarization from runaway star bowshocks-I. The general case

source_fields.sections_text: Model: The paper proposes a three-stage methodology that uses 3D MHD simulations to model stellar wind-ISM interactions, followed by 2D electron transport and synchrotron polarization calculations to produce realistic radio polarization maps of supernova remnants. Results: The bowshock structure at $\\theta = 30^\\circ$ shows stronger nonthermal synchrotron emission in the inner region ($\\sim 4$ pc), with spectral indices as low as $-0.35$ and polarization degrees up to $70\\%$, while thermal Bremsstrahlung dominates the total intensity and reduces polarization to a maximum of $25\\%$ in the total emission maps. Conclusion: This study finds that radio polarization in runaway star bowshocks is strongly affected by inclination, magnetic field turbulence, thermal emission, and non-uniformities, leading to low polarization degrees (typically 8–10%) and making polarization a poor tracer of magnetic field direction, especially in the inner, high-intensity regions. Discussion: The experiment concludes that runaway massive star bowshocks can act as particle accelerators, with a maximum polarization degree of ~45% in ideal conditions and a most probable value of 8–10% in realistic scenarios, supporting the classification of such sources as Particle Accelerating Stellar bowshock Nebulae (PASBN), even when nonthermal emission is undetectable due to strong thermal components. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of identifying runaway massive star bowshocks as promising gamma-ray sources through predicted nonthermal inverse Compton emission, addressing the unresolved problem of particle acceleration in stellar wind–interstellar medium interactions and the potential role of these systems in Galactic cosmic ray production.

abstract: High velocity stars move through the interstellar medium with V > 30 km/s.
In this work we model the synchrotron emission of runaway massive star bowshocks aiming to produce synthetic radio emission and polarization maps for two frequencies: 1.40 and 4.86 GHz.
title: Radio polarization from runaway star bowshocks-I. The general case

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical modeling paper on synchrotron emission and polarization in runaway star bowshocks using MHD simulations. No observational data or object lists presented.

papers.title: Rapid formation of a very massive star >50000 $M_\\odot$ and subsequently an IMBH from runaway collisions. Direct N-body and Monte Carlo simulations of dense star clusters
papers.author_names: Marcelo C. Vergara Abbas Askar Albrecht W. H. Kamlah Rainer Spurzem Francesco Flammini Dotti Dominik R.G. Schleicher Manuel Arca Sedda Arkadiusz Hypki Mirek Giersz Jarrod Hurley Peter Berczik Andres Escala Nils Hoyer Nadine Neumayer Xiaoying Pang Ataru Tanikawa Renyue Cen Thorsten Naab

abstract.text: Context. We present simulations of a massive young star cluster using \\\\textsc{Nbody6++GPU} and \\\\textsc{MOCCA}. The cluster is initially more compact than previously published models, with one million stars, a total mass of $5.86 \\\\times 10^5~\\\\mathrm{M}_{\\\\odot}$, and a half-mass radius of $0.1~\\\\mathrm{pc}$.\\\n  Aims. We analyse the formation and growth of a very massive star (VMS) through successive stellar collisions and investigate the subsequent formation of an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) in the core of a dense star cluster.\\\n  Methods. We use both direct \\\\textit{N}-body and Monte Carlo simulations, incorporating updated stellar evolution prescriptions (SSE/BSE) tailored to massive stars and VMSs. These include revised treatments of stellar radii, rejuvenation, and mass loss during collisions. While the prescriptions represent reasonable extrapolations into the VMS regime, the internal structure and thermal state of VMSs formed through stellar collisions remain uncertain, and future work may require further refinement.\\\n  Results. We find that runaway stellar collisions in the cluster core produce a VMS exceeding $5 \\\\times 10^4~\\\\mathrm{M}_{\\\\odot}$ within 5 Myr, which subsequently collapses into an IMBH.\\\n  Conclusions. Our model suggests that dense stellar environments may enable the formation of very massive stars and massive black hole seeds through runaway stellar collisions. These results provide a potential pathway for early black hole growth in star clusters and offer theoretical context for interpreting recent JWST observations of young, compact clusters at high redshift.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes using the NBody6++GPU code, a highly parallelized direct N-body simulation method that employs hierarchical time-stepping, the Ahmad-Cohen neighbor scheme, and relativistic post-Newtonian dynamics to evolve dense star clusters and study their dynamical evolution, binary interactions, and gravitational wave sources. Results: Over a 5 Myr evolution, the system forms a VMS with a mass of $M_{\\text{VMS}} \\geq 1000\\,M_\\odot$ by 0.18 Myr, leading to an IMBH at $4.69\\,\\text{Myr}$ (N-body) and $4.48\\,\\text{Myr}$ (MOCCA), with 91.6% of collisions involving the VMS/IMBH—mostly binary (45.1%) or hyperbolic (46.5%)—and a total escaped mass reaching $20,000\\,M_\\odot$. Appendix#2: This section describes modifications to the SSE stellar evolution algorithm to improve numerical stability and consistency for massive stars in the very massive star (VMS) regime, with plans for future extensions to low metallicity and massive star formation. Appendix#3: This section discusses the limitations of the traditional rejuvenation mechanism in stellar collision models, particularly for low-mass stars, and proposes a corrected approach to accurately compute stellar ages and evolutionary phases after mass transfer. Appendix#4: This section describes modifications to the main sequence stellar radius function for very massive stars to prevent unphysical large radii, ensure numerical stability, and improve agreement with theoretical models by capping the radius and simplifying the radius evolution for masses above 100 solar masses. Appendix#5: The updated rejuvenation model (Eq. C.4) corrects the overestimation of rejuvenation in blue stragglers for $ q < 0.75 $ compared to the original treatment (Eq. C.2), as demonstrated by improved agreement with boundary conditions at $ q = 0 $ and $ q = 1 $ in Fig. E.1. Conclusion: This study demonstrates that stellar mergers in ultra-dense star clusters can efficiently form massive variable-mass stars (VMS) capable of growing to tens of thousands of solar masses, highlighting a promising pathway for the formation of intermediate-mass black holes and supermassive black hole seeds through a collisional process. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel semi-analytic model incorporating critical mass scaling and collisional dynamics in dense star clusters to explain the formation of intermediate-mass black hole seeds via runaway stellar collisions, addressing the long-standing problem of how massive black holes could form rapidly enough to seed supermassive black holes in the early universe. References#2: This section lists a comprehensive set of academic references spanning topics in astrophysics, including gravitational N-body simulations, stellar dynamics, binary star systems, and cosmological observations. Initial conditions: This section outlines the initial conditions of the star cluster model, including its size, density, metallicity, mass, and stellar population parameters, essential for simulating stellar evolution.

abstract: The cluster is initially more compact than previously published models, with one million stars, a total mass of $5.86 \\\\times 10^5~\\\\mathrm{M}_{\\\\odot}$, and a half-mass radius of $0.1~\\\\mathrm
We use both direct \\\\textit{N}-body and Monte Carlo simulations, incorporating updated stellar evolution prescriptions (SSE/BSE) tailored to massive stars and VMSs.
We find that runaway stellar collisions in the cluster core produce a VMS exceeding $5 \\\\times 10^4~\\\\mathrm{M}_{\\\\odot}$ within 5 Myr, which subsequently collapses into an IMBH.
Our model suggests that dense stellar environments may enable the formation of very massive stars and massive black hole seeds through runaway stellar collisions.
title: Rapid formation of a very massive star >50000 $M_\\odot$ and subsequently an IMBH from runaway collisions

catalog_assessment.evidence: Simulation paper using N-body and Monte Carlo methods to study runaway stellar collisions forming VMS and IMBH. No observational stellar data or catalogs.

papers.title: Disruptions of stars and binary systems on chaotic orbits in an axisymmetric Milky Way center
papers.author_names: Zephyr Penoyre Elena Maria Rossi Nicholas C. Stone

abstract.text: Non-spherical potentials allow a wide range of trajectories, both regular and chaotic, whose periapse distances can vary orbit to orbit. In particular chaotic trajectories can bring a system arbitrarily close to the central massive black hole leading to a disruption. In this paper, we work with an observationally benchmarked model of the innermost 200 pc of the Milky Way and show that low z-angular momentum trajectories are commonly chaotic. We compute the timescales and properties of close pericenter passages, and compare the implied collisionless disruption rate to the well-studied collisional rate from 2-body scatterings. We find that the relative collisionless rate can dominate by orders of magnitude. Our calculations are relevant for a wide range of disruption phenomena, including the production of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and tidal disruption events (TDEs). Most of these disruptions involve stars come from the Nuclear Stellar Cluster, with a pericenter distribution that strongly favours shallow encounters, and a preference for high inclination interactions. The latter implies that unbound disrupted material - whether ejected stars or stellar debris - would be preferentially directed towards the galactic poles. Many of our conclusions apply generally to any galaxy with a non-spherical galactic centre potential and central massive black hole.

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: Chaotic "diving orbits" with small $l_z$ are common in the inner Milky Way, accounting for ~30% of low-$l_z$ trajectories and causing disruptions (TDEs and hypervelocity stars) on timescales of 10–100 Myr, preferentially from near-perpendicular directions, with collisionless disruption rates dominating over collisional ones—especially for high tidal angular momentum systems—across a wide range of galactic models. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel mechanism by which stellar systems can be tidally disrupted by a Massive Black Hole in the Galactic Center due to the flattened potential inducing chaotic, non-conserved angular momentum orbits—enabling disruption without two-body scattering and providing a new pathway to explain TDEs, Hyper-Velocity Stars, and other near-encounter phenomena. Trajectories: This section identifies and analyzes trajectories in the galactic center potential that reach critical tidal angular momentum at periapse, potentially leading to disruption by the central supermassive black hole, using AGAMA to simulate and characterize their behavior in 3D phase space. Model Of The Galactic Center: The paper proposes a self-consistent galactic center model using a static density-potential pair, where the Nuclear Stellar Disk (NSD) and Nuclear Stellar Cluster (NSC) are defined by observed stellar actions and distribution functions, with a central supermassive black hole and bulge added post-iteration to maintain mass consistency and numerical stability. Motion In Axisymmetric Potentials: This section derives the dynamics of particles in axisymmetric gravitational potentials, showing how motion is governed by conserved energy and angular momentum, with the effective potential accounting for centrifugal effects and the evolution of angular momentum due to radial gradients in the potential. Rates Of Disruption For A Given System: This section estimates the disruption rates of stellar systems in a galactic potential by analyzing collisionless diving orbits and scattering events, focusing on the role of loss cone/wedge dynamics and the dominance of chaotic orbits in determining disruption rates. Probabilities, Properties And Timescale Of Diving Orbit Disruptions: The probability and timescale of diving orbit disruptions depend on a system's energy and $z$-angular momentum, with disruption likelihood averaged over chaotic orbit states under the assumption of uniform occupation in $l_{\\theta,p}, \\theta_p$ space, despite potential biases in the distribution favoring more disruptive trajectories.

abstract: Our calculations are relevant for a wide range of disruption phenomena, including the production of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and tidal disruption events (TDEs).

Most of these disruptions involve stars come from the Nuclear Stellar Cluster, with a pericenter distribution that strongly favours shallow encounters, and a preference for high inclination interactions
The latter implies that unbound disrupted material - whether ejected stars or stellar debris - would be preferentially directed towards the galactic poles.
title: Disruptions of stars and binary systems on chaotic orbits in an axisymmetric Milky Way center

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical study of disruption mechanisms producing hypervelocity stars in Milky Way center using dynamical modeling. Mentions HVS production but presents no observational catalog or object-level data.
